text:
Hi everyone, I’m Vlad currently SDE @ Amazon.
I’m fascinated by a cluster of things that the following labels approximately points at: Tools For thought, Cognitive Augmentation, Novel HCI approaches, future of programming, personal data infrastructure
Also “malleable software” as I quite enjoy building things that have an immediate impact on my experience of using computers.
And I’m interested in making it easier for people to do that/coming up with ways to make our environment more hackable in general.
Most of my popular OSS work is around enhancing the tools in the areas above (<https://github.com/roam-unofficial/roam-toolkit><https://github.com/roam-unofficial/roam-toolkit,|,> <https://github.com/Stvad/CrowdAnki> etc)
Another thing I find fascinating is DSLs (with focus on internal ones). I did a talk on what kind of delightful things you can do to Python to make it better suitable for writing DSLs (<https://twitter.com/VladyslavSitalo/status/1344539651818758144?s=20> )
-
I’m currently trying to figure out how to work on something within that cluster of things full time. So if people have recommendations for *companies they find fascinating in the area - I’d appreciate if you can share them :)*

text: In my opinion STEPS was a very tragic event in regards to real live impact

text: Alan -Kay always likes to talk about how important science is but as far as I can percieve it STEPS was a very poorly executed project. Of course sure there maybe where funding or other issues but IMO its simply not viable  to proceed like how they did , IF you _really_ care about your (research) idea.

text:
Hello Channel,

I am Shubhadeep. Indian by birth and presently living in Paris (moving to India in Feb 2021). I have been working as CTO of Codist for the last one year and half (EF pre-seed funded). We have been working on Machine Learning on Source Code and built amazing open source products including docly (Auto document your python code), tree-hugger (Universal Source Code mining platform), CodeBERT (a transformer model, pre-trained on code corpus).

Before that I have been working on Very Large Scale data Engineering and Machine Learning in Companies such as Kpler (Paris), Ubble (Paris), Mammoth Analytics (SF/Bangalore), Experience Commerce (presently part of Samsung) etc.

I have really wide range of experience and expertise. Some of them

- Vast ML and DL skills including clear understanding about the theory behind (Created product from scratch which is Open Source and used by more than 500 active users in less than three months)
- Data Engineering (Beam, Spark, Kafka, AWS, GCP, Elasticsearch)
- Dev-ops (Docker, K8S, ECS, Ansible)
- Programming Language (Python, Go, C++, Rust, Javascript)
- REST API, Scrapping, Architecture Design
- Building and maintaining a team, mentoring them
- Internationally published author in Data Science <https://www.packtpub.com/product/data-wrangling-with-python/9781789800111>
- Highly popular tech blogger - <https://medium.com/@rcshubha>

Some of my profiles / Other online presence -

Github - <https://github.com/rcshubhadeep>
Stackoverflow - <https://stackoverflow.com/users/1587118/src#>
Linkedin - <https://www.linkedin.com/in/shubhadeep-roychowdhury/>
Youtube - <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2d-w3cx2TlU&feature=youtu.be> (my talk on Machine Learning on Source Code in a very popular DS channel)

What am Looking for? -

With a heavy heart I have to bid goodbye to Codist. We did all we could. But despite being good engineers / scientists, we were not good sellers. So at the end we accepted the reality that we are out of money and no-one is yet ready to invest (Oh you, God d**n COVID!). So, I am looking to get a suitable senior position (CTO, Lead Data Scientist, Senior ML Engineer etc.)  in a well funded start-up.

I am ready to join whenever is good for you.

I will be fully remote (unless you have office in Goa, India)

Contractual or normal job both welcome. But I am mainly looking for financial  reward more than ESOP kind of things.

I have worked in remote first teams for a good part of my life, so don't worry I know what I am doing.

I can always adjust my schedule with you.

I will be travelling in Feb so there will be a period of about three weeks when I won't be working. (I can start after that, if that suits you better)

I am more than happy to have a virtual coffee with you if you think that is worth it. And I am looking forward to join an exciting organisation, solving a meaningful and challenging problem.

I am also attaching my CV with this message. And I wish you all a very happy new year ahead! :tada:

text: Did you read the github issue history? Dame Lang the person behind nile and gezira said he would need about 6000$ a month and since he did not believe this amount could have been funded through crowdfunding he went on to create some proprietary business analyst toolset.  I really do think we are missing understanding and organisations and institutions. Which really understand resilience and simplicity

text:
Lol, I was basically getting just-enough functionality out of the system to be able to solve the puzzles. For example, for one of the problems, I had to add Maps. Basically I finished up to Day 12 Part 1 before calling it quits, and the problems got too hard to keep up development and solving the problems at the same time, so I started using other languages.

One of the biggest bottlenecks was the type inference not being rock solid. One of the shortcuts I took was that if the editor couldn’t figure out the type, then the type resolved to “Any” which matches anything. But then the structured editor isn’t that useful because the list of autocomplete options is way too large.

Sooo now begins the rabbit hole of figuring out how to get a really nice type inference system. Currently investigating using VMware’s Differential Datalog engine for this...!

text:
I did read it. I deeply empathize with Dan Amelang b/c .. yeah building 5-6k recurring research funding is nearly impossible. Andy Matuschak seems to have kinda done it, but I believe he has a bigger following and it took 2 years I think just to get to “pay my mortgage” level. And the churn rate from his recent patreon email is pretty high. I’m friends with Nicky Case but he’s not doing research per say, instead crowdfunding more real world things like explorables & games (and also has a decent reach on social media).

Without an existing distribution channel, I don’t know how Dan would get the 6k needed to support his family

text:
The comparison with musicians is interesting. There's a limited sense in which music is inherently open source. You can just listen to it and, with enough skill, recreate it. It does take a lot more skill and equipment than compiling an open source project, or even lifting a technique from said project, but I think it lifts some of the pressure that inclines software people towards total transparency. Closed source software hides things we really need to look at.

While I enjoy having free software all over the place, I can't see where Jonathan is wrong. You get what you inventivize. Neither "CEOs" (rather broad brush there, btw) nor open source's simulated annealing approach are going to get us where we want to be.

I don't have anything like an answer, but here's one random idea: different financial/openness regimes for different layers of the stack, as well as different stages of development. Open source is working pretty well for basic libraries, for instance. We should keep doing that.

elements.text:
The comparison with musicians is interesting. There's a limited sense in which music is inherently open source. You can just listen to it and, with enough skill, recreate it. It does take a lot more skill and equipment than compiling an open source project, or even lifting a technique from said project, but I think it lifts some of the pressure that inclines software people towards total transparency. Closed source software hides things we really need to look at.

While I enjoy having free software all over the place, I can't see where Jonathan is wrong. You get what you inventivize. Neither "CEOs" (rather broad brush there, btw) nor open source's simulated annealing approach are going to get us where we want to be.

I don't have anything like an answer, but here's one random idea: different financial/openness regimes for different layers of the stack, as well as different stages of development. Open source is working pretty well for basic libraries, for instance. We should keep doing that.

text:
I don't find either of Jonathan's arguments particularly convincing.

On the first article, I don't see anything special about 1996. The "pre-1996" list includes LISP - a language from 1958! If we are allowed to reach 40 odd years in the past, then a lot of the 1996 list can be included in a 2020 list. It also oddly _excludes_ C++ from the description; _"useful incremental improvements on top of the foundational technologies that came before"_, despite it's name being a literal pun on incrementally improving C.

The second article, I don't really follow what type of organisation is expected to be producing groundbreaking research. Small software houses making UI widgets? From my understanding, most significant steps forward have come from monopolies, academia, or military. (this is where most of the 1996 list items came out of). He says research can't be done at a startup, so what research is actually being hindered by OSS?

I read a lot of what <@U6KQ2S410> writes, but I can't follow a coherent line of reasoning here. Perhaps they are more just meant to stimulate debate :shrug:
